ITER VV Lateral Rattle — Research Findings

Generated 2026-05-27 · 5 000-sample MC · scipy HiGHS LP · served via imas-ink

Note on imas-ink: plots on this page were generated via imas-ink-repl (persistent matplotlib REPL) and served live through imas-ink-serve_chart. imas-ink's REPL is not limited to plasma physics — it provides a stateful Python/numpy/matplotlib environment for any scientific plotting.

1 — Problem Statement

The ITER Vacuum Vessel rests on 9 gravity supports at R = 8 m, equally spaced. Each support has a toroidal pin/hinge that allows free radial motion but blocks toroidal motion, with a 3 mm total gap (±1.5 mm) per support.

During installation, each support lands at a random position within its gap (independent, driven by manufacturing and placement tolerances). The assembly team asks: from any given assembly condition, how much can the VV subsequently move? This "rattle" is the worst-case additional rigid-body translation within the remaining gaps.

2 — Physics Model

Kinematic matrix

Three rigid-body DOFs: (dx, dy, dθ). The 9×3 kinematic matrix A:

A[i,:] = [−sin φᵢ,  cos φᵢ,  R]    where φᵢ = π/2 − 2πi/9, R = 8 m

The third column is exactly R = 8 for all supports (verified analytically and numerically).

Rattle LP (correct formulation)

max  [cos θ, sin θ, 0] · δq
s.t.   A·δq ≤  1.5mm − uᵢ    (forward gap remaining)
      −A·δq ≤  1.5mm + uᵢ    (backward gap remaining)

The uᵢ values are the original as-landed support positions, not the lstsq-projected rigid-body state. This is the critical correctness requirement — see §4.

Rattle magnitude = max over θ ∈ [0, 2π). Solved by scipy HiGHS (exact LP).

3 — Updated Numbers

Absolute LP bounds (nominal assembly, u = 0)

QuantityValueMethod
Max VV centre displacement X±1.547 mmLP (HiGHS) — feasible ✓
Max VV centre displacement Y±1.523 mmLP (HiGHS) — feasible ✓
Max VV rotation θ±0.1875 mradAnalytic (δ/R = 1.5/8 000)
Rotation contribution at R = 3 m±0.563 mm0.1875e-3 × 3 000 mm
Max displacement at R = 3 m (combined)≤ 2.11 mmTranslation + rotation
WRONG — pseudoinverse1.920 mm (infeasible)τ_max = 2.086mm > 1.5mm limit

MC rattle statistics (5 000 samples, correct LP)

StatisticAny direction+X only+Y only
Mean1.522 mm0.772 mm0.769 mm
Median1.514 mm0.775 mm0.773 mm
P751.838 mm1.109 mm1.100 mm
P902.119 mm1.357 mm1.345 mm
P95 (key)2.282 mm1.830 mm1.815 mm
P992.556 mm1.534 mm1.516 mm
Worst observed (n=5000)2.896 mm1.547 mm1.523 mm
Absolute theoretical max3.094 mm1.547 mm1.523 mm
Key numbers for the assembly team:
  • From the nominal (centred) assembly: VV can move ±1.547 mm X, ±1.523 mm Y
  • In 95% of random assembly states: worst rattle ≤ 2.282 mm in any direction
  • Absolute worst case (theoretical): 3.094 mm (VV assembles at one LP extreme, rattles to opposite)
  • Best case (alternating state): 0 mm (fully locked)
  • The P95 +X rattle (1.830 mm) exceeds the nominal LP +X bound (1.547 mm) because an assembly state with negative mean toroidal displacement leaves more forward gap

4 — Bugs Found and Fixed

FileBugImpactStatus
bounding_box_analysis.py Pseudoinverse: max displacement via unconstrained τ. Allows τ_max = 2.086mm > 1.5mm 24% overestimate (1.920mm vs 1.547mm true LP) Not yet fixed
iter_vv_monte_carlo.py Samples 9 independent u_i then projects via lstsq — computes assembly position PDF, not rattle Answers wrong question; does not model rattle Superseded
vv_rattle_mc.py (implement-rattle ver.) Used formula M(ψ) − ψᵀq₀ which is only valid when u ∈ Col(A). Alternating case → 1.547mm (should be 0) Catastrophically wrong for non-rigid assembly states Overwritten
vv_rattle_mc.py (current) — Correct LP. Verified: alt=0.000mm ✓, nominal=1.547mm ✓ Correct

5 — If We Physically Measured the Gaps

What measurement would give us

Currently we use MC because we don't know where the VV will land. If, after installation, someone measures the actual pin offset uᵢ at each of the 9 supports (e.g. with feeler gauges or dial indicators), the analysis collapses to a single deterministic LP: 

known u → g⁺ = 1.5mm − u,  g⁻ = 1.5mm + u
→ one LP solve per direction → exact rattle envelope

No MC uncertainty. The assembly team gets the precise rattle budget for this installation, not a probability distribution. This is always the preferred analysis if measurements are available.

Measurement accuracy requirements

From Figure 3: a 0.1 mm measurement uncertainty per gap propagates to approximately ±0.056 mm uncertainty in the rattle estimate (σ). This is very small relative to the rattle magnitude (2–3 mm). Gap measurement accuracy of 0.1 mm is more than adequate; even 0.3 mm accuracy would give <0.2 mm rattle uncertainty.

6 — Could We Perform a Better Analysis?

With gap measurements (strongly recommended)

ImprovementWhat to doBenefit
Exact rattle bounds Measure all 9 gaps after installation, run single LP Deterministic — replaces MC entirely for that installation
Risk assessment Compare each remaining gap g⁺ᵢ, g⁻ᵢ to identify near-consumed supports Identifies which supports are close to binding — useful for load analysis
Optimise assembly Adjust support positions during installation to minimise rattle (iterative shimming) Could approach the alternating-locked configuration → near-zero rattle

Without measurements (improving the MC)

ImprovementWhat to doExpected impact
Realistic gap distribution Replace Uniform(−1.5, +1.5) with Normal(0, σ) where σ comes from machining tolerance stack-up Likely reduces P95/P99 (gaps cluster near zero in practice)
Correlated gaps If adjacent supports tend to land similarly (same assembly jig), introduce spatial correlation Could increase or decrease rattle depending on correlation sign
Larger MC 20k–100k samples for better P99.9 tail statistics Reduces statistical uncertainty on P99 estimate (currently ~50 samples above P99)
Include deformation Add VV self-weight sag and thermal gradient effects as an offset to effective gap Could shift effective assembly position systematically
Optimisation over direction Replace 48-direction scan with exact LP over direction (bilinear → parametric LP) More accurate rattle magnitude; eliminates discretisation error (~0.02 mm)
Recommendation: Gap measurement (feeler gauge, ±0.1 mm) after installation is by far the highest-value improvement. It eliminates the entire MC uncertainty and gives the assembly team an exact clearance budget rather than a distribution. The measurement analysis is a one-liner: solve the LP once with the measured gaps. The MC analysis (current work) provides the pre-installation planning bound when measurements are not yet available.

7 — Remaining Work

  1. Fix bounding_box_analysis.py — replace pseudoinverse with scipy LP in find_extreme_position()
  2. Update main.py — single entry point calling vv_rattle_mc.main()
  3. Larger MC run — 20k samples for reliable P99 tail (currently ~50 samples > P99)
  4. Displacement at attachment radii — envelope at R = 1, 3, 5 m for component clearance checks
  5. Assembly team deliverable — standalone HTML report (clean, no code history)
